meta.description: One platform for the island. Economy, districts, elections, disasters, and public services — with provenance on every number. Built for Sri Lankans, by Sri Lankans.

vanni.title: Vanni electoral crosswalk
vanni.body: Sri Lanka uses separate administrative and electoral district boundaries. Four Northern administrative districts — Kilinochchi, Mannar, Mullaitivu, and Vavuniya — are aggregated into a single Vanni electoral district for parliamentary elections and some presidential reporting.
vanni.districtNote: Presidential results shown here reflect the combined Vanni electoral district tally published by the Election Commission, not an independent count for this administrative district alone.

brand.tagline: Built for Sri Lankans, by Sri Lankans.
brand.taglineShort: Built for Sri Lankans, by Sri Lankans
brand.mission: Sri Lanka's all-in-one civic intelligence platform
brand.builtBy: We believe every Sri Lankan deserves transparent, accessible public data — without visiting dozens of separate websites.

about.title: Mission & data sources
about.subtitle: Open civic intelligence for Sri Lanka — aggregated from public sources with provenance on every metric.
about.missionBody: Lankawa unifies public civic data — economy, disasters, districts, and more — into one accessible platform. We believe Sri Lankans should not need to visit dozens of separate websites to understand their country.
about.freshnessBody: Live metrics show a freshness tier based on when the source was last successfully fetched:
about.freshnessFresh: Fresh — fetched within the expected cadence
about.freshnessStale: Stale — older than expected but still available
about.disclaimerBody: Lankawa aggregates data from public and partner APIs. It is not an official government publication. Always verify critical information with primary sources.
about.apiBody: All data is available via a versioned JSON API with OpenAPI documentation.
about.platformBody: All data, documentation, and source attribution live on Lankawa. You never need to leave this platform to explore metrics, understand methodology, or use the public API.

footer.built: Built for Sri Lankans, by Sri Lankans
footer.copyright: Aggregated from public sources. Not an official government publication.

sources.title: Data Sources
sources.subtitle: Every metric on Lankawa traces back to a registered source with freshness and methodology.

developers.overviewBody: All endpoints are same-origin JSON APIs. No API key is required for read access. Responses include freshness tiers and internal provenance paths.

suggestions.flood: How many flood stations are active?

learn.title: How to read Lankawa
learn.subtitle: Plain-language guides to the numbers you see — no jargon, no spin. We explain what we show, where it comes from, and what we do not know.
learn.trustPreamble: Lankawa is not a government agency. These guides explain our methodology so you can decide when to trust a number and when to verify with the primary source.

fxRates.title: How USD/LKR exchange rates work
fxRates.summary: Why Lankawa shows CBSL selling rates, what buy/sell means, and why your bank may differ.
fxRates.inlineHint: The headline USD/LKR figure is the Central Bank selling rate — not every counter or remittance quote.
fxRates.intro: Every morning Sri Lankans check the dollar rate. Lankawa pulls the official daily table from the Central Bank of Sri Lanka (CBSL) and shows the selling rate as the headline number.
fxRates.demoIntro: This is the same shape as the pulse card on Home and Economy — with both buy and sell rates visible.
fxRates.trustNote: We do not predict tomorrow's rate, recommend a money changer, or guarantee that any commercial counter will match CBSL's published table.
sections: CBSL publishes a daily reference table with separate buying and selling rates for the US dollar. Banks and licensed money changers use these as a benchmark, but they may add margins, fees, or inventory limits.

Lankawa's headline USD/LKR value is the CBSL selling rate — the rate at which the central bank would sell dollars to licensed dealers on that business day. The buying rate (shown in the card note) is what dealers receive when selling dollars back.

Rates update on CBSL's business-day schedule, not continuously like a forex trading screen. A Fresh badge means we fetched the latest published table within our expected cadence; Stale means the table is older than expected but still displayed.
If you are remitting, importing, or settling a large invoice, compare the CBSL reference with your bank or exchange counter — Lankawa is a civic reference, not a quote for your transaction.

demo.buyHint: What dealers receive when selling USD to CBSL
demo.footnote: Illustrative values — live numbers appear on Home and Economy.
demo.step3: You verify at your counter if needed

floodLevels.title: What river flood alert levels mean
floodLevels.summary: NORMAL, ALERT, WARNING, and DANGER — how water height maps to station thresholds.
floodLevels.inlineHint: Alert colours reflect river height against station-specific thresholds — not evacuation orders.
floodLevels.intro: Lankawa aggregates river gauge readings from the public lk-flood-api, which tracks Irrigation Department and DMC-linked stations. Each station has its own threshold lines.
floodLevels.demoIntro: A station card compares current water level (metres) against alert, warning, and danger thresholds set for that river reach.
floodLevels.trustNote: River gauges are one input for preparedness. They do not replace DMC bulletins, local authority warnings, or on-the-ground judgment during heavy rain.
sections: Water level is measured in metres above a station datum — essentially how high the river is at that gauge point, not how deep floodwater is in your street.
NORMAL means the level is below the station's alert threshold. ALERT means the level crossed the first threshold — increased monitoring, not necessarily public danger.
WARNING and DANGER are progressively higher thresholds for that specific station. A WARNING at one river does not mean the same risk at another — each gauge has different geography.
Remarks and timestamps come from the upstream feed. If history is unavailable, sparklines degrade gracefully — we show what we have and mark freshness honestly.

levels.alert: First threshold crossed — watch forecasts and upstream rain.
levels.warning: Significant rise — prepare according to local guidance.
levels.danger: Highest station threshold — follow DMC and local authority instructions.

freshness.title: How to read freshness badges
freshness.summary: Fresh, Stale, and Down — what each tier means before you act on a number.
freshness.inlineHint: Every live metric carries a freshness tier so stale data is never silent.
freshness.intro: Lankawa compares the last successful fetch time against each source's expected update cadence. Badges appear on pulse cards, source pages, and the status dashboard.
freshness.demoIntro: The badge on a metric tells you how recently we confirmed the upstream source — not whether the underlying event happened.
freshness.trustNote: Fresh does not mean "correct for every use case" — only that the source responded recently. Down means we could not reach the source; we may show a cached or seed fallback elsewhere with clear labelling.
sections: Fresh: the source responded within its expected cadence (for example, CBSL FX within roughly a business day, flood levels within about ten minutes to an hour depending on upstream).
Stale: data is older than expected but still available. Useful for context — verify before time-sensitive decisions.
Down: the adapter failed or the upstream API is unreachable. Check /status for the last error and whether a fallback is in use.
Badges describe our pipeline health, not official urgency levels. A Fresh flood reading can still be alarming; a Stale FX rate may still be the latest CBSL table.

demo.asOf: As of last successful fetch · illustrative

fresh.body: Fetched within the expected cadence for this source.

stale.body: Older than expected — upstream may be delayed or cached.
stale.action: Use for context; verify if timing matters.

down.action: Open /status or the source page before relying on the number.

provenance.title: Where every number comes from
provenance.summary: From pulse card to registered source to upstream publisher — full attribution chain.
provenance.inlineHint: Tap any metric's provenance link to see methodology, cadence, and last fetch time.
provenance.intro: Every metric on Lankawa maps to an entry in our source registry. We document adapter type (API, scheduled scrape, or partner feed), update cadence, and live health.
provenance.demoIntro: Follow the chain from the number you see to the organisation that publishes the underlying data.
provenance.trustNote: We aggregate and normalise — we do not originate official statistics. Election results, budgets, and health surveillance should be confirmed with the publishing authority when used in formal contexts.
sections: Pulse metrics link to /sources/[id] pages with methodology text, adapter type, and freshness history.
API integrations (flood API, Octane fuel, Open-Meteo weather) fetch structured JSON. Scrapes (CBSL FX, CEB power) parse public pages on a schedule.
Partner feeds (PropertyLK, Vehicle platform, Life/Ariva) attempt live calls with honest fallback to seed data when upstream is unavailable — see the seed vs live guide.
The public /api/v1/* JSON API exposes the same provenance paths developers see in the UI — no hidden sources.

demo.previewMetric: USD / LKR · CBSL daily exchange rates

metric.label: On Lankawa
metric.title: Pulse card or module table
metric.body: You see the value, unit, observed time, and a freshness badge.

source.label: Source registry
source.title: /sources/cbsl_fx
source.body: Methodology, cadence, adapter type, and last successful fetch.

upstream.label: Upstream publisher
upstream.title: Central Bank of Sri Lanka
upstream.body: The organisation that originates the data — linked from the source page.

seedVsLive.title: Live data vs illustrative seed
seedVsLive.summary: Which modules fetch live feeds today — and which use representative seed data.
seedVsLive.inlineHint: We label seed and fallback data clearly so approximations are never mistaken for live official series.
seedVsLive.intro: Lankawa ships with both live adapters and curated seed datasets. Seed modules let the platform stay usable while ingest pipelines are built — we document limitations openly in /about and on each module.
seedVsLive.demoIntro: A honest snapshot of what is live today versus representative seed — check /status for current health.
seedVsLive.trustNote: Seed data is never presented as live surveillance or official ledger extracts. When an adapter falls back to seed, freshness and source pages reflect that.
sections: Live today: CBSL FX, Octane fuel, lk-flood-api levels, Open-Meteo weather, CEB power checks, CSE market board (with cache), and news RSS — subject to upstream availability.
Representative seed: dengue weekly tables, MP scorecards, tender notices, local government directory completeness, historical election approximations, and cost-of-living composites.
Mixed: property and vehicle modules attempt partner API calls but fall back to seed bands when upstream errors — watch freshness and source notes on those pages.
We expand live coverage as adapters stay green for 30+ days — see docs/ROADMAP.md. If a module matters to you, /status shows whether it is actually live right now.

demo.liveBody: Fetched from an upstream API or scheduled scrape on a public cadence. Freshness badges apply.
demo.seedBody: Curated sample aligned with published formats — useful for UI and demos, not operational response.

fx.name: USD / LKR
fx.note: CBSL daily table via scrape + DB ingest

dengue.name: Dengue cases
dengue.note: Representative weekly seed — not live surveillance

property.name: Property medians
property.note: Partner API when healthy; seed bands otherwise

tenders.name: Tender feed
tenders.note: Static seed notices — not live e-GP

costOfLivingMethodology.title: Cost of living methodology
costOfLivingMethodology.subtitle: How Lankawa builds the district composite index — and what it is not.
costOfLivingMethodology.inputProperty: PropertyLK district median land bands, Colombo-normalized for the property component.
costOfLivingMethodology.inputFood: FoodLK or Ariva Life essentials basket when available; otherwise seed food basket estimates.
costOfLivingMethodology.inputCoconut: Coconut is extracted from the food staple items as a visible COL food signal, with live, life_federation, or seed provenance.
costOfLivingMethodology.methodBody: When live federation inputs are available, Lankawa refreshes the national petrol reference and rescales district food baskets from the essentials estimate. Property components use live PropertyLK medians relative to Colombo. The Coconut Index surfaces the coconut staple price used by the food signal. District ranks come from a blended index (seed structure + live components). If partners are offline, the published seed snapshot is shown with seed provenance.
costOfLivingMethodology.limitsBody: This is not official NCPI, CCPI, or Department of Census and Statistics HIES data. It is a civic comparison tool with provenance badges. Do not use it for wage setting or formal inflation claims.

homeDistrict.title: Your district
homeDistrict.subtitle: Pin a home district for faster morning checks — saved on this device only.

landChange.title: Land Change Pulse
landChange.subtitle: District greenery and built-up change since 2018 — a morning-sized land signal, not a satellite gallery.
landChange.attribution: Lankawa indices are our own district aggregation for civic pulse use. Research prior art: Team Watchdog satellite2024 (MIT). Not official Survey Department land use — do not treat as legal zoning.
